content:
# Luke Chapter 4 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 88-89 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_004_page_001.jpg, LUK_004_page_002.jpg

---

**1.** And Jesus, full of [the] Holy Spirit, returned from the Jordan, and was being led by the Spirit in the wilderness

**2.** [for] forty days, being tempted by the devil. And he ate nothing in those days, and when they were completed, he hungered.

**3.** And the devil said to him, "If you are [the] Son of God, speak to this stone that it might become bread."

**4.** And Jesus answered to him, "It has been written that, 'Not upon bread alone shall [the] man live.'"

**5.** And leading him up, he showed him all the kingdoms of the inhabited-world in a moment of time.

**6.** And the devil said to him, "To you I will give all this authority and their glory, because to me it has been delivered, and to whomever I wish I give it.

**7.** You therefore, if you worship before me, it shall all be yours."

**8.** And answering, Jesus said to him, "It has been written, 'You shall worship [the] Lord your God, and him alone you shall serve.'"

**9.** And he led him into Jerusalem, and set him upon the pinnacle of the temple, and said to him, "If you are [the] Son of God, cast yourself down from here;

**10.** for it has been written that, 'He shall command his angels concerning you, to guard you,'

**11.** and that, 'Upon [their] hands they shall bear you up, lest at-any-time you strike your foot against a stone.'"

**12.** And answering, Jesus said to him, "It has been said, 'You shall not put-to-the-test [the] Lord your God.'"

**13.** And having completed every temptation, the devil departed from him until a season.

**14.** And Jesus returned in the power of the Spirit into Galilee; and a report went-out through the whole surrounding-region concerning him.

**15.** And he himself was teaching in their synagogues, being glorified by all.

**16.** And he came into Nazareth, where he had been brought-up, and he entered according to his custom on the day of the sabbaths into the synagogue, and stood-up to read.

**17.** And [the] scroll of the prophet Isaiah was given-over to him, and having unrolled the scroll, he found the place where it was having-been-written:

**18.** "[The] Spirit of [the] Lord [is] upon me, on-account-of-which he anointed me to proclaim-good-news to [the] poor; he has sent me to proclaim release to [the] captives, and recovery-of-sight to [the] blind, to send-forth [the] broken [ones] in release,

**19.** to proclaim [the] acceptable year of [the] Lord."

**20.** And having rolled-up the scroll, giving [it] back to the attendant, he sat-down; and the eyes of all in the synagogue were gazing at him.

**21.** And he began to say to them, "Today this scripture has been fulfilled in your ears."

**22.** And all were bearing-witness to him, and were marveling at the words of grace proceeding out of his mouth, and they were saying, "Is not this [the] son of Joseph?"

**23.** And he said to them, "Surely you will say this parable to me: 'Physician, heal yourself; as-many-things-as we heard having-happened in Capernaum, do also here in your fatherland.'"

**24.** And he said, "Amen I say to you that no prophet is acceptable in his fatherland.

**25.** But upon truth I say to you, many widows were in the days of Elijah in Israel, when the heaven was shut-up for three years and six months, as a great famine came upon all the land,

**26.** and to none of them was Elijah sent, except to Zarephath of Sidon, to a woman [who was a] widow.

**27.** And many lepers were in Israel in [the time of] Elisha the prophet, and none of them was cleansed, except Naiman the Syrian."

**28.** And all were filled with anger in the synagogue, hearing these [things],

**29.** and having risen-up, they cast him out, outside of the city, and led him unto [the] brow of the hill upon which their city had been built, so-as to cast him down-the-cliff.

**30.** But he, having passed through [the] midst of them, was going [his way].

**31.** And he went-down into Capernaum, a city of Galilee, and was teaching them on the sabbaths.

**32.** And they were astonished at his teaching, because his word was with authority.

**33.** And in the synagogue there was a man having a spirit of an unclean demon, and he cried-out with a great voice,

**34.** "Ha! What [is there] to us and to you, Jesus Nazarene? Have you come to destroy us? I know you, who you are — the Holy [One] of God."

**35.** And Jesus rebuked him, saying, "Be silenced, and come-out from him." And the demon, having thrown him into the midst, came-out from him, having harmed him nothing.

**36.** And astonishment came upon all, and they were speaking-together to one another, saying, "What [is] this word, that with authority and power he commands the unclean spirits, and they come-out?"

**37.** And a report was going-forth concerning him into every place of the surrounding-region.

**38.** And having risen-up out of the synagogue, he entered into the house of Simon. And the mother-in-law of Simon was being-held by a great fever, and they asked him concerning her.

**39.** And having stood-over above her, he rebuked the fever, and it left her; and immediately, having risen-up, she was serving them.

**40.** And [while the] sun was setting, all — as-many-as had [those who were] ailing with various diseases — brought them to him; and he, having laid [his] hands upon each one of them, was healing them.

**41.** And demons also were coming-out from many, crying-out and saying, "You are the Son of God." And rebuking [them], he was not permitting them to speak, because they knew him to be the Christ.

**42.** And [when] day came, having gone-out, he went into a desolate place; and the crowds were seeking him, and they came up-to him, and were restraining him [from] going from them.

**43.** But he said to them, "Also to the other cities it is necessary [for] me to proclaim-the-good-news [of] the kingdom of God, because for this I was sent-forth."

**44.** And he was proclaiming in the synagogues of Judea.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **4:1 — "by the Spirit" (en to pneumati):** Sinaiticus reads the dative construction, consistent with being led "in/by" the Spirit. The phrase describes continuous action during the forty days.

2. **4:4 — Shorter reading:** Sinaiticus omits "but upon every word of God" (alla ep' panti rhemati theou), which is present in the Byzantine/Majority text tradition. The Sinaiticus reading ends simply with "Not upon bread alone shall the man live." This is a significant variant shared with Vaticanus (B).

3. **4:5 — Order of temptations:** Sinaiticus (with Vaticanus and most Alexandrian witnesses) places the temple-pinnacle temptation third (vv. 9-12), differing from Matthew's order. This is the Lukan sequence, not a scribal alteration.

4. **4:8 — "Get behind me, Satan" omitted:** Sinaiticus omits the phrase hypage opiso mou Satana ("Get behind me, Satan") found in some later manuscripts before "It has been written." This shorter reading is shared with Vaticanus and is considered original by most text-critics.

5. **4:17 — "having unrolled" (anaptuxas):** Sinaiticus reads anaptuxas ("having unrolled"), consistent with a scroll format. Some manuscripts read anoixas ("having opened"), reflecting later codex-book conventions.

6. **4:18 — Longer Isaiah quotation:** Sinaiticus includes the full citation from Isaiah 61:1-2 with "to heal the broken-hearted" (iasasthai tous suntetrimmenous ten kardian) — though this phrase is disputed; some Sinaiticus correctors and Vaticanus omit it. The first hand of Sinaiticus appears to include the fuller reading.

7. **4:27 — "Naiman" (Naiman):** Sinaiticus spells the name of the Syrian commander as NAIMAN rather than the more common Neeman/Naaman. This reflects a variant Greek transliteration of the Hebrew name.

8. **4:29 — "cast him down-the-cliff" (katakremnisai auton):** The verb katakremnizo is vivid — "to throw down a precipice." Sinaiticus preserves this strong term without softening.

9. **4:34 — "Ha!" (Ea):** Sinaiticus reads the interjection EA, an exclamation of alarm or protest. Some manuscripts omit it. Translated as "Ha!" to preserve the raw emotive force.

10. **4:41 — "him to be the Christ" (auton einai ton Christon):** Sinaiticus reads that the demons "knew him to be the Christ." Some manuscripts add "Jesus" or vary the phrasing. The Sinaiticus reading identifies the reason for silencing: their knowledge of his messianic identity.

11. **4:44 — "Judea" (Ioudaias):** Sinaiticus reads "the synagogues of Judea" rather than "the synagogues of Galilee" (found in the Byzantine tradition). This is a notable Alexandrian reading shared with Vaticanus. "Judea" here likely functions as a broad term for the Jewish land, not the southern province specifically, since the narrative context is Galilee.

12. **4:35 — "having harmed him nothing" (meden blapsas auton):** Sinaiticus explicitly notes that despite the violent throwing, the demon caused no injury — an intentional Lukan detail emphasizing Jesus' protective authority.

13. **4:44 — verse count:** Luke 4 contains 44 verses in the standard versification. Sinaiticus does not mark verse divisions (these are later editorial additions), but the text flow corresponds to the standard 44-verse structure.
